title: Solar Still
summary: A solar still uses sunlight to evaporate moisture from soil, vegetation, or contaminated water, then collects the condensed pure water in a container. Originally developed by USDA researchers and field-tested by the U.S. Air Force, the pit-type solar still is documented in the U.S. Army Survival Manual (FM 21-76) and by the U.S. Geological Survey. While it can remove salts, heavy metals, and pathogens that boiling alone cannot, an improvised solar still typically produces only about 240 mL (8 oz) per day in arid conditions — far less than the 3.8 liters a person needs daily. It should be considered a supplemental or last-resort water procurement method, not a primary source.
quickAnswer: A solar still distills small amounts of pure water using sunlight and a plastic sheet, but its output is extremely low (roughly one cup per day in dry conditions) and it should never be relied upon as a sole water source.
whenToUse: All other water purification methods (boiling, chemical treatment, filtration) are unavailable
Available water is brackish, saline, or contaminated with dissolved salts or heavy metals that boiling cannot remove
Stranded in a remote area with no clean water source and clear plastic sheeting is available
As a supplemental water source to extend limited water supplies in a prolonged survival situation
preferredAction: Use boiling, chemical treatment, or filtration as your primary water purification method whenever possible — a solar still is a last-resort supplement, not a replacement.
backupAction: If no other method is available and you have clear plastic sheeting, construct a pit-type solar still in a location with the most available ground moisture (such as a dry creek bed or depression), and add non-toxic vegetation to increase yield.
warnings: A solar still produces very little water — typically about 240 mL (one cup) per day in arid desert conditions, which is roughly 6% of a person's minimum daily water needs in hot environments.
Do not rely solely on a solar still if other safer water sources or purification methods are available.
Building a pit solar still in hot conditions may cause you to lose more water through sweat during construction than you collect over the first several days of operation.

Solar distillation does NOT remove volatile organic chemicals such as fuel, pesticides, or solvents. Never use a solar still to purify water contaminated with petroleum products or chemical pollutants — the toxins will evaporate and recondense in your collection container.
The still requires direct sunlight to function. Output drops dramatically on cloudy days and is zero at night.
A torn or punctured plastic sheet renders the still nonfunctional. The plastic sheet has no natural substitute and must be carried as a preparedness item.
Neither FEMA nor the EPA include solar stills in their standard emergency water purification recommendations. The technique is primarily documented in military survival and field-practice literature.
whatNotToDo: Do not add radiator fluid, antifreeze, or any liquid containing ethylene glycol to a solar still — the toxins will vaporize and poison the collected water.
Do not disassemble the still repeatedly to check water levels, as this releases trapped humidity and significantly reduces output. Use a sip tube instead.
Do not rely on a solar still as your only water source in a desert survival situation — the output cannot sustain life in hot conditions.
Do not assume distilled water from a solar still is safe if the source water was contaminated with volatile chemicals (fuel, solvents, pesticides).
Do not place the still in a shaded area. It requires full, direct sunlight for maximum efficiency.
redFlags: Signs of severe dehydration: no urination for 8+ hours, extreme thirst, very dry mouth, dizziness, confusion, or rapid heartbeat.
Any sign of heat stroke while constructing or tending the still: high body temperature, altered mental state, nausea, hot dry skin.
Collected water has an unusual chemical smell or taste, which may indicate volatile contaminant carryover.
Still output has dropped to near zero for multiple consecutive days despite sunny conditions, indicating the soil moisture source is exhausted.
preparednessTips: Carry a 6x6-foot sheet of clear plastic (such as a painter's drop cloth) in your emergency kit — this is the one essential item with no natural substitute.
Include 6 feet of flexible plastic tubing (fish-tank style) for drinking without dismantling the still.
Store at least one gallon of water per person per day as your primary emergency supply. A solar still is a backup, not a plan.
Learn to identify your local area's natural moisture sources: dry creek beds, depressions, areas with green vegetation at the surface.

Consider adding a commercial portable solar still (such as the Aquamate) to maritime or desert survival kits — these produce 0.5 to 2.0 liters per day, significantly more than an improvised pit still.

images.description: Cross-section diagram of an improvised pit-type solar still showing: the dug pit (approximately 3 feet deep by 3-4 feet wide), a collection container centered at the bottom, clear plastic sheet draped over the top and secured at the edges with rocks or soil, a small rock in the center of the sheet creating a drip point directly above the container, optional sip tube running from the container to outside the pit, and arrows indicating sunlight entering through the plastic, moisture rising from the soil, and condensation dripping down into the container. Clean line-diagram style on a neutral background.
images.caption: Cross-section of an improvised pit solar still
images.altText: Diagram showing a pit in the ground covered with plastic sheeting, with condensation dripping into a central collection container

sourceReferences.title: How to Build Your Own Solar Still
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Federal agency reference providing basic solar still construction concept and illustration.

sourceReferences.title: US Army Survival Manual FM 21-76
sourceReferences.whyUseful: The standard military survival reference that documents the pit-type solar still as a water procurement method, including construction details and tips to increase yield.

sourceReferences.title: Clinical Practice Guidelines on Water Treatment for Wilderness, International Travel, and Austere Situations: 2024 Update
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Peer-reviewed 2024 clinical guidelines covering solar disinfection methods, providing medical context for solar-based water treatment approaches.

sourceReferences.title: Emergency Disinfection of Drinking Water
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Provides context that solar stills are not included in standard EPA emergency water purification recommendations, supporting the guide's positioning as a last-resort method.

sourceReferences.title: Solar Water Distillation Fact Sheet
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Explains the unique advantage of solar distillation in removing salts and heavy metals, and the critical limitation that volatile organic chemicals are not removed.

sourceReferences.title: Desert Survival: How to Build a Solar Still
sourceReferences.whyUseful: Detailed field-practice reference documenting the USDA origins of the solar still, construction materials, and the critical warning about radiator fluid.
